title: Castle in the Air - Chapter Two: In which Abdullah is mistaken for a young lady (Fragment 4)
text:
Flower-in-the-Night considered this offer, swaying dubiously back and forth with her hands clasped around her knees. Abdullah could almost see rows of fat, bald men with gray beards passing in front of her mind‚Äôs eye.

‚ÄúI assure you,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthat men come in every sort of size and shape.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen that would be very instructive,‚Äù she agreed. ‚ÄúAt least it would give me an excuse to see you again. You‚Äôre one of the nicest people I‚Äôve ever met.‚Äù

This made Abdullah even more determined to come back tomorrow. He told himself it would be unfair to leave her in such a state of ignorance. ‚ÄúAnd I think the same about you,‚Äù he said shyly.

At this, to his disappointment, Flower-in-the-Night got up to leave. ‚ÄúI have to go indoors now,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúA first visit must last no longer than half an hour, and I‚Äôm almost sure you‚Äôve been here twice as long as that. But now we know each other, you can stay at least two hours next time.‚Äù

‚ÄúThank you. I shall,‚Äù said Abdullah.

She smiled and passed away like a dream, beyond the fountain and behind two frondy flowering shrubs.

After that the garden, the moonlight, and the scents seemed rather tame. Abdullah could think of nothing better to do than wander back the way he had come. And there, on the moonlit bank, he found the carpet. He had forgotten about it completely. But since it was there in the dream, too, he lay down on it and fell asleep.

He woke up some hours later with blinding daylight streaming in through the chinks in his booth. The smell of the day before yesterday‚Äôs incense hanging about in the air struck him as cheap and suffocating. In fact, the whole booth was fusty and frowsty and cheap. And he had an earache because his nightcap seemed to have fallen off in the night. But at least, he found while he hunted for the nightcap, the carpet had not made off in the night. It was still underneath him. This was the one good thing he could see in what suddenly struck him as a thoroughly dull and depressing life.

Here Jamal, who was still grateful for the silver pieces, shouted outside that he had breakfast ready for both of them. Abdullah gladly flung back the curtains of the booth. Cocks crowed in the distance. The sky was glowing blue, and shafts of strong sunlight sliced through the blue dust and old incense inside the booth. Even in that strong light, Abdullah failed to discover his nightcap. And he was more depressed than ever.

‚ÄúTell me, do you sometimes find yourself unaccountably sad on some days?‚Äù he asked Jamal as the two of them sat cross-legged in the sun outside to eat.

Jamal tenderly fed a piece of sugar pastry to his dog. ‚ÄúI would have been sad today,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúbut for you. I think someone paid those wretched boys to steal. They were so thorough. And on top of that, the Watch fined me. Did I say? I think I have enemies, my friend.‚Äù

Though this confirmed Abdullah‚Äôs suspicions of the stranger who had sold him the carpet, it was not much help. ‚ÄúMaybe,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúyou should be more careful about whom you let your dog bite.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot I!‚Äù said Jamal. ‚ÄúI am a believer in free will. If my dog chooses to hate the whole human race except myself, it must be free to do so.‚Äù

After breakfast Abdullah looked for his nightcap again. It was simply not there. He tried thinking carefully back to the last time he truly remembered wearing it. That was when he had lain down to sleep the previous night, when he was thinking of taking the carpet to the Grand Vizier. After that came the dream. He had found he was wearing the nightcap then. He remembered taking it off to show Flower-in-the-Night (what a lovely name!) that he was not bald. From then on, as far as he could recall, he had carried the nightcap in his hand until the moment when he had sat down beside her on the edge of the fountain. After that, when he recounted the history of his kidnapping by Kabul Aqba, he had a clear memory of waving both hands freely as he talked, and he knew that the nightcap had not been in either one. Things did disappear like that in dreams, he knew, but the evidence pointed, all the same, to his having dropped it as he sat down. Was it possible he had left it lying on the grass beside the fountain? In which case‚Äî

Abdullah stood stock-still in the center of the booth, staring into the rays of sunlight, which, oddly enough, no longer seemed full of squalid motes of dust and old incense. Instead, they were pure golden slices of heaven itself.

‚ÄúIt was not a dream !‚Äù said Abdullah.

Somehow his depression was clean gone. Even breathing was easier.

‚ÄúIt was real !‚Äù he said.

He went to stand thoughtfully looking down at the magic carpet. That had been in the dream, too. In which case‚Äî

‚ÄúIt follows that you transported me to some rich man‚Äôs garden while I slept,‚Äù he said to it. ‚ÄúPerhaps I spoke and ordered you to do so in my sleep. Very likely. I was thinking of gardens. You are even more valuable than I realized!‚Äù

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Two: In which Abdullah is mistaken for a young lady

vocabulary.definition: someone who accepts a particular idea or philosophy
vocabulary.example: Not I! said Jamal. I am a believer in free will.

vocabulary.definition: so bright as to dazzle or temporarily blind
vocabulary.example: He woke up some hours later with blinding daylight streaming in through the chinks in his booth.

vocabulary.definition: the sound roosters make early in the morning
vocabulary.example: Cocks crowed in the distance. The sky was glowing blue.

vocabulary.definition: causing a feeling of sadness or gloom
vocabulary.example: It was still underneath him. This was the one good thing he could see in what suddenly struck him as a thoroughly dull and depressing life.

vocabulary.definition: the feeling of sadness or displeasure caused by the non-fulfilment of one's hopes or expectations
vocabulary.example: At this, to his disappointment, Flower-in-the-Night got up to leave.

vocabulary.definition: in a doubtful or uncertain manner
vocabulary.example: Flower-in-the-Night considered this offer, swaying dubiously back and forth with her hands clasped around her knees.

vocabulary.definition: pain in the ear
vocabulary.example: And he had an earache because his nightcap seemed to have fallen off in the night.

vocabulary.definition: to throw or move something suddenly and with force
vocabulary.example: Abdullah gladly flung back the curtains of the booth.

vocabulary.definition: the ability to make choices independently
vocabulary.example: I am a believer in free will. If my dog chooses to hate the whole human race except myself, it must be free to do so.

vocabulary.definition: having or covered with fronds (large palm-like leaves)
vocabulary.example: She smiled and passed away like a dream, beyond the fountain and behind two frondy flowering shrubs.

vocabulary.definition: unpleasantly warm and stuffy, often with a bad smell
vocabulary.example: The whole booth was fusty and frowsty and cheap.

vocabulary.definition: smelling stale, damp or stuffy; old-fashioned
vocabulary.example: The whole booth was fusty and frowsty and cheap.

vocabulary.definition: lack of knowledge or information
vocabulary.example: He told himself it would be unfair to leave her in such a state of ignorance.

vocabulary.definition: providing useful or interesting information or insight
vocabulary.example: Then that would be very instructive, she agreed. "At least it would give me an excuse to see you again."

vocabulary.definition: the unlawful carrying away of a person against their will
vocabulary.example: When he recounted the history of his kidnapping by Kabul Aqba.

vocabulary.definition: tiny particles or specks
vocabulary.example: Rays of sunlight, which no longer seemed full of squalid motes of dust and old incense.

vocabulary.definition: to tell someone about something; to give an account of an event or experience
vocabulary.example: After that, when he recounted the history of his kidnapping by Kabul Aqba, he had a clear memory.

vocabulary.definition: beams or rays of light
vocabulary.example: Shafts of strong sunlight sliced through the blue dust and old incense inside the booth.

vocabulary.definition: extremely dirty and unpleasant, especially as a result of poverty or neglect
vocabulary.example: Staring into the rays of sunlight, which, oddly enough, no longer seemed full of squalid motes of dust and old incense.

vocabulary.definition: completely motionless
vocabulary.example: Abdullah stood stock-still in the center of the booth.

vocabulary.definition: causing difficulty in breathing especially through lack of fresh air or presence of heat
vocabulary.example: The smell of the day before yesterday‚Äôs incense hanging about in the air struck him as cheap and suffocating.

vocabulary.definition: beliefs or feelings that someone is guilty of wrongdoing without proof
vocabulary.example: Though this confirmed Abdullah‚Äôs suspicions of the stranger who had sold him the carpet.

vocabulary.definition: lacking excitement or interest; dull
vocabulary.example: After that the garden, the moonlight, and the scents seemed rather tame.

vocabulary.definition: in a gentle, caring, or affectionate way
vocabulary.example: Jamal tenderly fed a piece of sugar pastry to his dog.

vocabulary.definition: to carry or move from one place to another
vocabulary.example: It follows that you transported me to some rich man‚Äôs garden while I slept.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that is difficult or impossible to explain
vocabulary.example: Tell me, do you sometimes find yourself unaccountably sad on some days?

vocabulary.definition: worth a lot of money or very important
vocabulary.example: You are even more valuable than I realized!

vocabulary.definition: very bad or unpleasant
vocabulary.example: I think someone paid those wretched boys to steal.
